title: Earth, Our Home
description: The story of Earth is the story of life. It is also a story of loss, one worsened by climate change. But while the climate crisis can be a story of devastation, the work being done to mitigate it, to adapt to it, brings hope. And reminds us, our future is in our hands. The verses in this book tell these stories, in the voices of the orangutan, the Hasdeo Arand forest, the Gangetic dolphin and the Pondicherry shark.

pages.text:
The Earth's Story
I am Earth, your home.
A place to explore and roam,
A place of wonder and love,
With soil beneath and sky above.
Choking on carbon, gasping for breath,
Now is the time to protect
Earth, your home,
A place of wonder and love.

The Earth’s climate is changing! Our planet is now warmer, because humans are pumping more and more greenhouse gases into the atmosphere and, at the same time, cutting down more and more trees. Glaciers and ice caps are melting, the sea level is rising and we are seeing extreme weather events, such as floods and droughts, more frequently.

Hasdeo Arand: Hear My Story
by Karthika Naïr
Greetings, young ones who may not know my name.
Age-old forest, I do not ask for fame!
Lung of the earth I am, and of your land.
My breath gives shape to river, ravine, strand
and quicksand, tribe and tree, bird, beast, et al.
Life I grant to aam, bamboo, mahua, sal…
scores of herbs and scrubs and medicinal plants.
Gods and Gonds, tigers, leopards, even ants,
elephants, bears, and many more do roam
on my chest. Since ancient times, I’m their home.
Yes, home. Green and brown, golden, auburn: theirs
through summer whirlwinds, earthquakes,
monsoon scares,
through joy, sorrow, the ravages of time.
Together, yes, like a bell and its chime.

Hasdeo Arand: Now Do
Something, Please
Together we thought we would always be.
But humans found black gold in my belly.
A billion metric tonnes of coal, they say,
run in my veins. For this fortune, they’ll slay —
oh, gladly!
—
the fates of plants and seedpods,
tigers and tribes, elephants, even gods.
Know this: they tear into my flesh to reach
the heart as I speak, deaf to my pained screech,
blind to the hundreds of thousands of trees
marked for death, one by one. Do something, please.
Look, listen, query, while there is still light.
Act, before it’s too late. Act, for my plight
turns the sky to dust and rivers to ash.
Act, and save yourselves from the Earth’s backlash.

Forests like Hasdeo Arand in Chhattisgarh, which is home to leopards, elephants and sloth bears, are being cleared for coal mining. Trees absorb carbon from the air, are reservoirs of water and help stabilize the climate. Cutting down forests adds more carbon to the atmosphere!

The Orangutan’s Call
by Salil Chaturvedi
Orange fur up high in the trees
Resting a moment in a rainforest breeze
A pause to look at the river afar
Nibbling a leaf like a chocolate bar
Gathering clouds in the afternoon
Under a leaf-umbrella I’ll be, soon
Then for a while in my leaf nest I’ll doze
A little rest for my four curly toes
Now what’s that smoke, it’s making me sneeze!

The Orangutan’s Sigh
Orange flames around the forest coil
Rows of trees aflame, even the soil
A fire so big, where do I go?
Near and far is a fiery glow
Gone my nest and my favourite trees
Ugly and horrific, someone stop this please!
Torching forests that were once our home
All for some palm oil, and a bit of gold
No, you won’t go far if the forests boil!

Rainforests in Indonesia and Malaysia are being cut down for oil palm cultivation. The orangutan and other animals’ homes are being threatened by habitat degradation and frightening wildfires. Changes in the climate increase the risk and severity of forest fire.

The Dolphin Dance
by Anushka Ravishankar
Shushuk shushuk
I’m a freshwater dolphin
Shushuk shushuk
I swim and I leap
Shushuk shushuk
I lurk in the shallows
Shushuk shushuk
But I hunt in the deep.
Shushuk shushuk
In the dark muddy waters
Shushuk shushuk
I see little light
Shushuk shushuk
But my ears are my eyes
Shushuk shushuk
For sounds give me sight.
Shushuk shushuk
As I breathe on the surface
Shushuk shushuk
I spring and I dive
Shushuk shushuk
And as long as I’m living
Shushuk shushuk
The river will thrive.

The Dolphin Lament
There’s sewage in the water
There’s garbage all around
Boats and dams and blockages
And fishing nets abound.
You hunt me for my flesh and oil,
You churn and chop the river
With noises that can make me blind.
Soon, I’ll be gone forever.
My shushuk breath no longer heard —
I will not dive nor leap.
The water will lose all its wealth;
The river, then, will weep.

The South Asian River Dolphin was once found in many river systems. This freshwater mammal is now endangered because of poaching, habitat loss and pollution. Clean rivers can reduce the impact of global warming.

The Pondicherry Shark
Says Hello
by Sampurna Chattarji
Aren’t I a looker, with my black-tipped fins!
With round eyes that gleam of beautiful things
Like my gentle nature, my freshwater soul
My charming gill-slits as I rock and roll
Down the rivers Saigon, Menik Ganga and Hooghly
Flashing my stripes and throwing a googly
With a notch in my tail like a real moon
The cutest crescent-shaped reason to swoon
With the sweetest and toothiest smile
As I say, “Hello Human, it’s been a while!
I’m Carcharhinus hemiodon
But if that’s a mouthful, just call me Gone.”
I dined on inkfish and became a poet
If I don’t tell you, how will you know it?
I’m a ghost — as friendly in life as in death
And I’m saying hello with my very last breath…

The Pondicherry
Shark Says Goodbye
I’m small, I’m stocky, I’m dead.
I rise to speak inside your head.
Once, we were a regular catch
But none of us was ever a match
For your tricks, your greed, your skill
At trapping and netting and making a kill.
You turned us into shark-fin soup
You threw us all into a mighty loop
You ruined our homes and our ease
By spilling oil on our rivers and seas.
After forty years they found me — dead.
I rise to speak inside your head.
Don’t you see, our lives are linked?
Save other sharks from going extinct.
Do your bit for all — it’s now or never.
From me, my friend, it’s goodbye, forever.

The Pondicherry Shark was considered extinct for almost 40 years, but was sighted once again in the waters of Sri Lanka. Critically endangered, the species needs protection from overfishing, habitat loss and pollution. Climate change is accelerating extinction. When a species goes extinct, entire ecosystems fall apart, affecting the balance of nature.

Our future, Our choice
Climate change’s impact is felt by human beings, but also by animals, insects, birds, plants, and all forms of life.
Scientists believe that the Earth has entered its sixth extinction period, one accelerated by climate change and human actions. But, we still have a choice. A choice for a better future.
Small steps can go a long way in protecting life on planet Earth, including ours. Start with the most important one — respecting and loving nature. From this respect and love will emerge voices that speak for Nature, and from these voices will grow responsible actions and green policies that make meaningful change happen.
Together we can face the climate crisis and build a better tomorrow. For you and everyone who calls planet Earth their home.
